title: _safe_float DEBUG log fires on every numeric coercion — production DEBUG runs will flood Railway
summary: `_safe_float` is called 54+ times in metrics.py and 111+ times across analytics-service. Every NaN/Inf scalar OR coercion failure now emits a DEBUG record (two new logger.debug sites). On a real analytics run, sanitize_metrics walks the full metrics_json/sibling-kind payload and re-coerces every numeric — many of which are legitimately None upstream (insufficient data windows). Once DEBUG is flipped on in production for incident triage, this hot path will produce hundreds of records per `compute_all_metrics` call. The choice of DEBUG over WARNING is correct (the comment acknowledges normal-path None), but the volume needs a guard.
evidence: _safe_float at L173-L201 emits DEBUG inside both the NaN/Inf branch (L181) AND the TypeError/ValueError branch (L196). Across analytics-service: 111 call sites (grep `_safe_float`); within metrics.py: 54 call sites. sanitize_metrics (L210-L222) recursively re-coerces nested dicts/lists — one `compute_all_metrics` produces dozens of legitimately-None scalars. Each produces a DEBUG record.
fix: Gate the DEBUG logs with `if logger.isEnabledFor(logging.DEBUG):` to skip the type(value).__name__ formatting when DEBUG is off — OR drop the helper-level logs entirely and rely on the inline WARNING sites that already name the failing scalar. The added DEBUG noise duplicates information already captured at the calling site WARNINGs (e.g. var_1d_95, cvar) without adding triage value.

title: 11 inline qs.stats try/except blocks duplicate the _safe_qstats_scalar helper pattern
summary: The file already exports a `_safe_qstats_scalar(name, fn, returns, returns_len)` helper at L86 that encodes exactly the WARNING-with-context pattern this PR adds inline 11 times for var_1d_95, cvar, gini, omega, gain_pain, tail_ratio, smart_sharpe, smart_sortino, and profit_factor. The dispatch table `_QSTATS_SINGLE_ARG_SCALARS` (L45) consumes the helper for the 8 NEW qstats scalars. Pre-sweep, these inline sites pre-dated the helper. Post-sweep, the obvious next step is to migrate them to the same dispatch-table pattern — instead, the PR codifies the divergence with 11 near-identical try/except + WARNING blocks that drift independently (e.g., gain_pain says 'qstats scalar gain_pain' while gini says 'qstats scalar gini'; both are 6 lines of boilerplate that the helper handles in 1 call).
evidence: _safe_qstats_scalar at L86-L105 is the canonical site. The inline blocks at L347-L350, L355-L358, L389-L392, L397-L400, L405-L408, L413-L416, L443-L446, L451-L454, L459-L462, L473-L476 are 1:1 mappable. The non-qs sites (np.percentile var_1m_99, Series.skew, Series.kurtosis, outlier ratios) genuinely don't fit the helper signature and would remain inline.
fix: Extend the dispatch table to include the 8 single-arg qs.stats scalars (var_1d_95, cvar, gini, omega, gain_pain, tail_ratio, smart_sharpe, smart_sortino, profit_factor) and replace the 8 inline blocks with `metrics_json[key] = _safe_qstats_scalar(key, getattr(qs.stats, attr), returns, returns_len)`. Note: var_1d_95 takes `cutoff=0.05` as a kwarg — that one site stays inline OR the helper signature gains `**kwargs`. The 3 non-qs sites (var_1m_99/skewness/kurtosis) and the outlier-ratios block remain inline since they don't share the single-arg shape.

title: Inner WARNING uses exc_info=True; outer ERROR at exchange.py:621 does not — observability asymmetry
summary: Every new WARNING site in this PR uses `exc_info=True` to attach the traceback (Binance L555-L562, Bybit L614-L617). But the OUTER wrapper `except Exception as e: logger.error('fetch_daily_pnl failed: %s', str(e))` at L620-L621 does NOT. The cascade test `test_binance_fallback_failure_escapes_to_outer_error` asserts BOTH logs fire, but only checks exc_info on the inner WARNING. When the cascade path fires (futures-income + fallback both raise), the operator gets a tracebacked WARNING and a tracebackless ERROR — useful for the first hop, less useful for the SECOND failure mode (the fallback failure) which is the one that actually escaped the inner handler.
evidence: exchange.py:620-621 — `logger.error('fetch_daily_pnl failed: %s', str(e))` (no exc_info). Compare to exchange.py:555-562 — inner Binance WARNING uses `exc_info=True`. Pre-existing in main (not introduced by this PR), but the cascade is newly load-bearing in the silent-failure sweep contract.
fix: Change L621 to `logger.error('fetch_daily_pnl failed: %s', str(e), exc_info=True)` and add an assertion in `test_binance_fallback_failure_escapes_to_outer_error` that the outer ERROR also carries exc_info. Cheap, symmetric, and pins the contract.

title: returns_len_for_log = len(returns) if returns is not None else None is dead-defensive — compute_all_metrics is unreachable with returns=None
summary: The signature of compute_all_metrics is `returns: pd.Series` (not Optional), and earlier code at L268 (`returns.isna()`), L295 (`returns.resample`), and L313 (`returns.tail()`) all dereference `returns` unconditionally — so `returns=None` would have crashed long before reaching L346. The `if returns is not None else None` guard is dead. Same observation appears 3 other times in the file (L548 drawdown, L598/L632 benchmark wrappers — pre-existing).
evidence: metrics.py:225-227 signature `returns: pd.Series`. metrics.py:268 `returns.isna()` unconditional. The first None-check is at L346 — far too late to be load-bearing.
fix: Drop the guard: `returns_len_for_log = len(returns)`. If the project ever wants to support `returns=None`, that's a signature change motivating an audit of every dereference, not a per-log workaround. Cosmetic but flags muddled-intent that a future refactor will need to untangle.

title: Per-scalar `# fail-soft: optional scalar.` comments duplicate the section-level docstring
summary: Each scalar try block now has its own single-line comment `# fail-soft: optional scalar.` directly above it (12 instances at L345, L353, L362, L378, L388, L396, L404, L412, L424, L434, L444, L463). The big docstring at L333-L345 already explains the failure-soft rationale once. The per-scalar repetition adds line noise without info — and creates drift risk when a future maintainer reads the per-scalar comments first and assumes they encode site-specific intent (especially the outlier-pair comment at L549 which DOES carry unique 'they degrade together' rationale).
evidence: metrics.py:345, 353, 362, 378, 388, 396, 404, 412, 424, 434, 444, 463 — 12 instances of the same single-line comment. The section docstring at L333-L345 covers the pattern.
fix: Delete the per-scalar single-line comments; keep only the section docstring and the site-specific comments (the outlier-pair comment at L549, the np.percentile note at L383-L385, the skewness/kurtosis notes at L429/L439). Saves 11 lines and reduces 'comments diverging from code' churn risk in future /simplify passes.

title: test_compute_all_metrics_outlier_ratios_failure_logs_warning patch leaks beyond the outlier block — robust by accident
summary: The test patches `pd.Series.mean` globally with a bool-dtype trigger. Today the ONLY bool-Series.mean() calls inside compute_all_metrics happen in the outlier block (L566-L567 of metrics.py), so the patch fires exactly once and the test passes. But the test is one-line-of-code away from breaking: if anyone adds a `(some_returns > 0).mean()` call ANYWHERE earlier in compute_all_metrics (a perfectly normal pattern for computing hit rate, win rate, etc.), the patch will trigger that site first and the outlier WARNING will NEVER fire. The test would then fail with a confusing 'outlier ratios failed' missing assertion — diagnosing it would require knowing the patch is dtype-gated.
evidence: test_metrics.py:1115-1125 — `_mean_raises_on_bool_series` triggers on bool dtype only. metrics.py grep for `\.mean()` shows L464, L466, L469, L471, L485-L486, L487 (all numeric Series), then L562 mean(), L566-L567 (THE bool sites), L586 (numeric). Today's coverage works. Tomorrow's new `(returns > X).mean()` site breaks it.
fix: Tighten the trigger: instead of dtype-gating, make the patch fire on a STACK-FRAME-LEVEL marker — e.g., assert the caller's filename + line is in the outlier block. OR change the test approach: monkeypatch a more localized symbol like `pd.Series.std` (which has only one bool-relevant call) and trigger on that. The bool-mean trick is clever but fragile.

title: Live gini AttributeError is now logged on EVERY analytics run — fail-loud worked, but the underlying drift is unaddressed
summary: `qs.stats.gini` does not exist on the pinned quantstats==0.0.81 — `test_compute_all_metrics_gini_qstats_attribute_missing_logs_warning` documents this as a live failure mode. Pre-sweep this was silently swallowed; post-sweep every analytics run now emits a WARNING. That's the intended fail-loud behavior — but the right next step is to (a) remove the dead call OR (b) implement a manual gini OR (c) pin a quantstats version that exposes it. None of those happen here. The PR succeeded in surfacing the regression; the regression itself remains. Worth flagging because the new test PINS this WARNING as a contract — future cleanup that removes the dead line will need to also remove the test, which couples them in a way a /simplify pass might miss.
evidence: metrics.py:390-396 — `qs.stats.gini(returns)` raises AttributeError unconditionally on quantstats==0.0.81 (requirements.txt). New test at test_metrics.py:1051-1075 pins the WARNING contract. Confirmed locally: `hasattr(qs.stats, 'gini') == False`.
fix: Choose one: (a) Delete the gini try/except + remove the test (acknowledge the gini metric is unavailable on the pinned qs version); (b) implement gini manually (it's a standard inequality coefficient — `2 * sum((i+1) * sorted_vals[i]) / (n * sum(sorted_vals)) - (n+1)/n`); (c) bump quantstats. Without one of these, every Railway run logs the same WARNING, which trains operators to ignore it — defeating the fail-loud purpose.
